title: practical english usage - Part 162
content:
CONTENTS     OF THIS  SECTION G   See you on next Monday. > 214.2 G   We   must discuss about your plans. ►    214.1 G   Who  ’ s the woman dressed with green? >213 ©     She doesn’ t want to depend of her parents. >213 ©     I’ m  not bad in tennis. > 213 ©     What time do we  arrive to Cardiff? > 213 ©     I like walking under the rain. >212 She looks much younger on this picture. > 212 O Q   Try to see it after my  point of view. >212 G   I don't like the idea of to get married. >211 G   I’ m  concerned about if you're ill. > 210.4 ©     I’ m  worried where she is. ►    210.4 had run away. > 210.3 G   He said the parents were responsible for that the child G   The  judge paid a lot of attention that the child was unhappy. >210.3 I knew about that he had problems. > 210.1 o Q   In my  family, about money was never spoken. > 209.7 G   To  where shall I send it? ►    209.7 G   For whom  is it, madam? ►    209.7 G   Which   period did it happen during? ►    209.6 G   I admired the patience she spoke with. > 209.6 G   You bought it? What money with? > 209.2 Do  you   know     what's    wrong     with    these,    and   why?

grammar •  209  prepositions at the ends of clauses with prepositions (►  237.3).

Because whom is unusual in an informal style, it is very rare in clauses that end That's what I'm afraid of She's the only woman (who) I've ever really been in love with.

You remember the boy (who) I was going out with?

This is the house (that) I told you about. (Less formal than ... about which I angry.) Joe's the person that I'm angry with. (Less formal than ... with whom I am also often goes at the end of the clause, especially in informal usage.

When a relative pronoun (►   233)  is the object of a preposition, the preposition 3 relative      clauses:     the    house       I told     you     about not With what money? (     What money with?) However, this structure is unusual when there is a noun with the question word.

What with?  Who for?

Some questions consist simply of question word + preposition.

Tell me what you're worried about. What a lot of trouble I'm in!

This also happens in indirect u//i-questions, and in other what-clauses.

What kind of films are you interested in?

Where did she buy it from? Which flight is the general travelling on?

What are you looking at? Who did you go with?

Who's the present for? (For whom is the present? is extremely formal.) comes at the end of the clause, especially in informal usage.

When  a question word is the object of a preposition, the preposition most often 2 wh-questions:        What      are    you     looking        at?

infinitive structures: The village is pleasant to live in.

passives: She likes to be looked at.

relative structures: Joe's the person that I'm angry with.

ivh-questions: Who's the present for?

especially in four cases: together with 'its' noun, adjective or verb at the end of the clause. This happens of a clause. In this case, the preposition does not always go with it - it may stay In some structures we may put the prepositional object at or near the beginning I'm really angry with Joe. They live in a small village.

This is a present for you.  He's looking at her.

after the preposition.

before it, and (2) a prepositional object - a noun phrase or pronoun that comes A  preposition often connects two things: (1) a noun, adjective or verb that comes 1  introduction 209 prepositions         at  th e  ends    of  dauses grammar •  209  prepositions at the ends of clauses For sentences like It’s got a hole in (it); 1 like cakes with cream on (them), ►    275.13.

For more information about formal and informal language, »281.

In my family, money was never spoken about, (     .. .about money was Prepositions cannot be moved away from passive verbs even in a formal style.

What did you buy that for? (          For what did you buy that?) but not What does she look like? (          Like what dees she look?) but not Where shall I send it to? (          To where shall I send it?) a fixed order.

And the structures where. . . to, what... like and what... for have not Who is it for, madam? (     For whom  is it?) questions which  have be as the main verb.
